[

1 control in a motion capture arena. In all these scenarios, the

v  1) Related works: There has been a growing number of
i
works addressing a variety of challenges related to compu-
X  tation, sampling and communication efficiency for resource-
r
a constrained systems. In the last decade,  the event-triggered
approach has been proposed as a way to minimize the use of
actuators [1]. These methods incorporate a triggering mecha-
nism which determines appropriate actuation or transmission
times by monitoring the state of the system. The impact of
such triggering mechanisms on the L2 gain is studied in [2],
[3], [4].
The concept of L2-consistent data transmission is intro-
duced and studied in [5], [6], [7]. A sequence of transmission

Fig. 1.  Encoder-decoder structure of the controller.
times is said to be L2-consistent if it performs at least as well
as a given reference periodic transmission scenario in terms
of both L2 gain and number of  transmissions. The finite
horizon setting is considered in [5] and an event-triggered
extension is proposed in [6], [7] for infinite horizons. In both
cases, a method based on dynamic games is proposed to find
L2-consistent transmission times.
All  these works consider that  the controller is either (i)
on the sensor side,  i.e.,  the control  input  is computed on
the sensor side and sent to the actuator when the triggering
condition is met; or (ii) on the actuator side, i.e., the state is
sent to the actuator where the control input is computed. In
contrast, a key distinguishing feature of our approach is that
the controller is split in two parts (an encoder and a decoder),
allowing for computations on both the sensor and the actuator
sides. We show that this controller structure allows to reduce
the number of transmissions even further.
The idea of encoder-decoder structure that splits a con-
troller  into a sensor-side and an actuator-side controller
appears  in  [8],  [9], where a method  to approximate a
linear controller  to accommodate given sensor-to-actuator
transmission times is proposed. This structure is also con-
sidered in our previous work [10] in the context of safety
control, where we show that the number of sensor-to-actuator
transmissions is given by the rank of the matrix mapping
the sequence of measurements to the sequence of control
inputs. Consequently, minimizing the number of transmis-
sions reduces to a rank minimization problem. Finally,  it
is shown in [10]  that  the encoder and decoder are given
by the causal  factorization of  that matrix. Also somewhat
related, in [11], several distributed agents implement a low-
rank time-invariant feedback gain to minimize a LQR cost
through limited broadcast communication. The goal in that
paper is not to minimize the number of transmission times
but rather to send compact messages at each time.
2) Contributions: We consider a discrete-time linear sys-
tem over  a finite horizon,  and we address  the problem
of finding a  linear  controller with memory  that  can be
implemented with a minimum number of sensor-to-actuator

To reduce the number of sensor-to-actuator transmissions,
we want to design a controller that can be implemented via
an encoder-decoder structure as proposed in [10] (see Fig. 1).
On  the sensor  side,  a  linear  encoder with a memory
computes messages mk based on the previous states xτ . The
message mk is transmitted at time tk to the decoder located
on the actuator side. The linear decoder computes control
inputs ut based on the messages previously received. For r
r

using the vectors d(t,k) ∈  . Importantly, this controller is
causal since every message is transmitted (i) after the states
it encodes have been measured, but (ii) before being used
to compute an input. The number of transmissions required

to implement this controller is the number r of transmitted
messages.

L2 gain smaller than a given γ ≥ 0, that is

Problem 1: Find  the minimal  r  such  that  there exist

Remark 1:  In [5], a sequence of transmission times is said
to be L2-consistent  for a period p if compared to the p-
periodic transmission,  it  leads to (i) a not  larger L2 gain,
and (ii) no more  transmissions. Note  that no encoder  is
considered in that work: when a transmission occurs,  the
state xt  is transmitted, which corresponds to nx messages.
If γp is the minimum L2 gain achievable with p-periodic
transmissions,  then solving Problem 1 with γ = γp in (3)
leads to L2-consistent transmission times for the period p.

In this section, we show that using techniques from [10],
Problem 1 can be reduced to a rank minimization over a
convex set. We note that while [10] considers safety over
bounded disturbances, we are interested in the case of a
bound on the L2 gain.  In Subsection III-A, Problem 1 is
reduced to a rank minimization problem over a non-convex
set;  then,  in Subsection III-B,  the system level  synthesis
framework [12] is used to make the constraints convex.

While Theorem 1 theoretically gives a method to solve
Problem 1,  it presents some numerical challenges. Indeed,
minimizing the rank is NP-hard and one  typically relies
on heuristics such as nuclear norm relaxation (which is the
tightest convex relaxation of the rank [13, Theorem 1]). This
num  num  num
leads to a numerical solution {Φx  , Φu  } for which Φu

is only numerically low rank,  i.e.,  it has many small (but
non-zero) singular values. These matrices lead to a controller
num  num  num −1
K  := Φu  (Φx  )  that is – at best – numerically low
rank as well.
To obtain a controller that requires a number of transmis-
sions equal  to the numerical rank, and not  the exact rank,
some approximation is needed. A usual way to find a low-
rank approximation of a matrix is via truncated singular value
num
decomposition (SVD). However a truncated SVD of K
would lead to a matrix that is not block-lower triangular and
for which no causal factorization exists.
This section aims to solve this issue. In Subsection IV-A,
we introduce the notion of approximate causal factorization
num
for which K  ≈ DE. Allowing for a factorization error
makes it possible to reduce the band below the (exact) rank
num
of K  . Then, in Subsection IV-B we derive an upper bound
on the degradation of the L2 gain in terms of the factorization
error.
A. Approximate causal factorization
In this subsection, we define formally the notion of ap-
proximate causal factorization (or ϵ-causal factorization) and
we provide an algorithm to compute it.
Definition 2 (ϵ-causal factorization): Let  X  ∈
R(T+1)nu×(T+1)nx
be  a  (nu, nx)-block-lower  triangular
R(T+1)nu×r
matrix. A pair  of matrices  (D, E)  ∈  ×
Rr×(T+1)nx
is an ϵ-causal factorization of X with band r
if they satisfy ∥X−DE∥ ≤ ϵ and Constraint 1 (Causality).
Note that a 0-causal factorization is a causal factorization as
defined in Definition 1.
As stated in Theorem 2 below, Algorithm 1 computes an
1
ϵ-causal factorization.  The algorithm works as follows: the
encoder matrix E is constructed row by row according to
the following rule: a row of X is added to E if and only if
it can not be approximated by a linear combination of the
rows already in E. When a row is not added, the coefficients
of the linear combination are added to the decoder D.

Note that the approximate causal factorization returned by
Algorithm 1 is not necessarily optimal when 0 < ϵ < ∥X∥,
in the sense that  there may exist another ϵ-causal  factor-
ization with a smaller band than the one returned. However,
minimizing the band is computationally hard since it involves
a combinatorial problem in selecting the transmission times
tk. We also note that  the optimal band is lower bounded

To illustrate our method,  we consider a two-dimensional
double integrator dynamics p¨x = ux and p¨y = uy discretized
using forward Euler method with time step 0.1. This gives
2  YTHON
A P  code  that  implements  our  method  and  generates
the  figures  is  available  at:  https://github.com/aaspeel/
Min-L2-Consistent. The reported computation times are obtained
using a laptop with an Apple M2 chip and 16 GB of RAM.

In this work, we addressed the problem of designing a
controller  that  requires a minimum number of sensor-to-
actuator transmissions while satisfying a bound on the L2
gain. To this end, we rely on causal factorization and system
level synthesis to reduce the problem to a rank minimization
over a convex set. To anticipate the fact  that optimization
methods yield matrices that are only numerically low rank,
we introduce the notion of approximate causal factorization
and an algorithm to compute it. Finally, the degradation in
L2 gain caused by the factorization error is bounded.
Our future works will consider an event-triggered exten-
sion suitable to the infinite horizon setting.
